title: Tensor
description: A multi-dimensional array of numbers that carries activations, weights, or gradients through the network.

whatItIs.title: What It Is
whatItIs.body: In deep learning, a tensor generalizes vectors and matrices to any number of dimensions. Frameworks such as PyTorch and JAX store model state and activations as tensors and run operations (matmul, softmax, layer norm) that preserve or reshape those dimensions.

whyItMatters.title: Why It Matters
whyItMatters.body: Shape errors are among the most common debugging surprises: a logits tensor might be [batch, vocab] while hidden states are [batch, seq, hidden]. Naming tensors by role (embeddings, keys, values) keeps papers, code, and this reference aligned.

simpleExample.title: Simple Example
simpleExample.body: A minibatch of 4 sequences, each 128 tokens wide, with hidden size 768 is often written as a tensor of shape 4×128×768. The language-model head may reduce the last dimension to vocabulary size, yielding 4×128×50257 logits before softmax.

whereItAppears.title: Where It Appears
whereItAppears.body: Tensors flow through every layer: embedding lookups, attention scores, feed-forward activations, loss computation, and optimizer updates. GPU kernels and compilation stacks are built around batched tensor programs.

commonConfusions.title: Common Confusions
commonConfusions.body: A tensor in ML is a numeric array, not the same as a tensor in general relativity. A single vector is a 1-D tensor; a matrix is 2-D. Scalars are 0-D tensors. Do not confuse tensor with parameter—parameters are tensors that get updated during training.

openingSummary: A tensor is an n-dimensional array with a shape like batch × sequence × hidden; embeddings, activations, and logits are all tensors at different ranks.
